original_hypothesis.title: Whitelisted Contract Gas Limit DoS via Unlimited Delegates
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: `ERC20MultiVotes` allows governance to whitelist contracts to bypass `maxDelegates` via `setContractExceedMaxDelegates`. If a whitelisted contract accumulates a massive number of delegates, any attempt to transfer or burn its tokens will trigger `_decrementVotesUntilFree()`, which loops over all delegates. This loop will exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking the contract's tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While this requires governance whitelisting, it represents a severe footgun for protocol integrations (e.g., yield aggregators or smart wallets) that could permanently lock user funds. Validation requires testing the gas cost of `_decrementVotesUntilFree` with hundreds of delegates.
deployment_assumptions: Governance whitelists a contract to exceed max delegates
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GuildToken.setContractExceedMaxDelegates and CreditToken.setContractExceedMaxDelegates functions exist to enable this.
preconditions: A contract is whitelisted to exceed maxDelegates
The contract delegates to a large number of addresses

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(N) loop over delegates during token transfer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker delegates to thousands of addresses on behalf of the contract (if the contract allows arbitrary delegation)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack to lock funds in a competitor's protocol integration.

economic_questions: How many delegates are required to hit the block gas limit?
Can the contract undelegate in smaller batches to recover?
validation_requirements: Measure gas cost per delegate in _decrementVotesUntilFree.

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users of the whitelisted integration contract
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of mobility for deposited tokens

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded loop gas DoS via unlimited delegates
validation_result.impact_summary: A governance-whitelisted contract that accumulates many delegates will have its tokens permanently locked because _decrementVotesUntilFree() materializes and loops over all delegates, exceeding the block gas limit during transfer/burn operations.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ERC20MultiVotes contract includes a critical security note: 'maxDelegates is a critical variable to protect against gas DOS attacks upon token transfer.' However, when governance whitelists a contract via setContractExceedMaxDelegates(), there is NO upper bound at all on the number of delegates that contract can accumulate. The _decrementVotesUntilFree() function first calls _delegates[user].values() which is O(N) — it materializes the entire EnumerableSet into a memory array regardless of whether the loop exits early. For a contract with thousands of delegates, just the memory allocation of values() (each SLOAD costs 2100 gas cold) plus the per-delegate loop operations (~30-50k gas each) will exceed the 30M block gas limit at approximately 600-1000 delegates. This means transfer(), transferFrom(), and _burn() will permanently revert. While the contract could theoretically call undelegate() one-at-a-time to reduce its delegate count, this depends entirely on the contract having such functionality exposed — a yield aggregator or smart wallet might not.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly present in the code. The SECURITY NOTE in ERC20MultiVotes explicitly acknowledges that maxDelegates protects against gas DOS, yet the canContractExceedMaxDelegates bypass provides no alternative upper bound. The _decrementVotesUntilFree function at ERC20MultiVotes.sol has an O(N) memory allocation via values() followed by an O(N) loop with expensive storage operations per iteration. GuildToken.sol and CreditToken.sol both expose setContractExceedMaxDelegates() as governance-callable functions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function is called during every transfer/transferFrom/burn. It materializes the ENTIRE delegate set into memory via values() (O(N) SLOAD operations), then loops with expensive per-delegate operations (SSTORE, checkpoint writes, events). For a whitelisted contract with hundreds or thousands of delegates, this will exceed the block gas limit, permanently preventing token movement.
When canContractExceedMaxDelegates[delegator] is true, there is absolutely no upper bound on how many delegates can be added. The maxDelegates check is completely bypassed with no alternative limit.
Every transfer calls _decrementVotesUntilFree, making transfers impossible when the delegate count is too high.

calling_sequence: 1. Governance calls setContractExceedMaxDelegates(contractAddr, true) to whitelist a contract
2. The whitelisted contract calls incrementDelegation() many times, adding hundreds/thousands of delegates with no upper bound
3. When the contract attempts transfer/transferFrom/burn, _decrementVotesUntilFree(user, amount) is invoked
4. _decrementVotesUntilFree calls _delegates[user].values() which allocates O(N) memory and performs O(N) cold SLOADs
5. The loop then performs O(N) expensive operations (SSTOREs, checkpoint writes, event emissions)
6. Total gas exceeds block gas limit (~30M), transaction reverts
7. Tokens are permanently locked in the contract
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: _decrementVotesUntilFree is the bottleneck. EnumerableSet.values() at line delegateList = _delegates[user].values() performs N cold SLOADs (2100 gas each). The loop body performs: 1 cold SLOAD for delegateVotes (~2100), 1 SSTORE to remove from set (~5000), 1 SSTORE for votes count (~5000), _writeCheckpoint with potential SSTORE (~20000), and an event emission (~1500). Per delegate: ~35-50k gas. At 600 delegates: ~21-30M gas, hitting the block gas limit.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Governance whitelists a contract to exceed max delegates
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that show specific whitelisting. The GIP_0 deployment proposal script does not contain whitelisting logic. However, GuildToken.setContractExceedMaxDelegates() and CreditToken.setContractExceedMaxDelegates() are exposed as governance-callable functions in the deployed contracts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is realistic for protocol integrations. A yield aggregator or smart wallet whitelisted by governance could accumulate delegates over time through normal usage. An attacker who controls or influences the whitelisted contract could deliberately delegate to many addresses. Since each incrementDelegation call only costs ~50-100k gas, accumulating 1000 delegates costs ~50-100M gas total (spread across many transactions), which is cheap relative to the permanent locking of all the contract's tokens. The attack can also be self-inflicted by a naive integration contract that doesn't understand the gas implications.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. Impact is permanent locking of all tokens held by the whitelisted contract. This could include significant TVL if the contract is a yield aggregator or protocol integration. The vulnerability requires governance whitelisting as a precondition, but once whitelisted, there is no safeguard against accumulating too many delegates. The protocol's own SECURITY NOTE acknowledges maxDelegates as critical for gas DOS prevention, yet the bypass mechanism removes this protection entirely.
recommended_mitigations: Add a separate, higher maximum delegate limit for whitelisted contracts (e.g., MAX_DELEGATES_FOR_CONTRACTS = 100) that is enforced even when canContractExceedMaxDelegates is true
Implement a paginated/batched version of _decrementVotesUntilFree that doesn't need to materialize the full delegate set
Add a view function that checks if a contract's delegate count is approaching dangerous levels, to allow monitoring
Consider using a lazy approach where delegates are not freed during transfer but instead tracked and settled separately
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GuildToken or CreditToken with a small maxDelegates (e.g., 5). 2. Deploy a test contract that can call incrementDelegation. 3. Whitelist the test contract via setContractExceedMaxDelegates(testContract, true). 4. Mint tokens to the test contract. 5. Have the test contract call incrementDelegation for 1000 different addresses with 1 wei each. 6. Attempt to call transfer from the test contract — measure gas and verify it exceeds block gas limit. 7. Verify that undelegate() still works individually as a potential recovery path.
uncertainties: The exact number of delegates at which gas limit is reached depends on EVM gas costs and block gas limit, which can change with network upgrades
Whether the whitelisted contract has a recovery mechanism (undelegate function) depends on its implementation
Whether governance would actually whitelist a contract that could accumulate many delegates
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of _decrementVotesUntilFree with varying delegate counts (100, 500, 1000, 5000)
Gas profiling of EnumerableSet.values() with large set sizes
Test recovery via batched undelegate calls from the whitelisted contract
Test interaction with ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree for GuildToken (both loops are called during transfer)
